analogy: Think of "Manufacturing Industries" through the chapter's own opening images: 58 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II Production of goods in large quantities after processing from raw materials to more valuable products is called manufacturing. Do you know that paper is manufactured from wood, sugar from sugarcane, iron and steel from iron ore and aluminium from bauxite?... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 58 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II, IMPORTANCE OF MANUFACTURING, 59 MANUFACTURING INDUSTRIES, 60 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Manufacturing Industries is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 58 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II Production of goods in large quantities after processing from raw materials to more valuable products is called manufacturing.

The text explains that do you know that paper is manufactured from wood, sugar from sugarcane, iron and steel from iron ore and aluminium from bauxite?

The text explains that do you also know that some types of clothes are manufactured from yarn which itself is an industrial product?

The text explains that people employed in the secondary activities manufacture the primary materials into finished goods.

The text explains that the workers employed in steel factories, car, breweries, textile industries, bakeries etc. fall into this category.

The text explains that some people are employed in providing services.

The text explains that in this chapter, we are mainly concerned with manufacturing industries which fall in the secondary sector.

The text explains that the economic strength of a country is measured by the development of manufacturing industries.

The text explains that iMPORTANCE OF MANUFACTURING Manufacturing sector is considered the backbone of development in general and economic development in particular mainly because– • Manufacturing industries not only help in modernising agriculture, which forms the backbone of our economy, they also reduce the heavy dependence of people on agricultural income by providing them jobs in secondary and tertiary sectors. • Industrial development is a precondition for eradication of unemployment and poverty from our country.

The text explains that this was the main philosophy behind public sector industries and joint sector ventures in India.

The text explains that it was also aimed at bringing down regional disparities by establishing industries in tribal and backward areas. • Export of manufactured goods expands trade and commerce, and brings in much needed foreign exchange. • Countries that transform their raw materials into a wide variety of finished goods of higher value are prosperous.

The text explains that india’s prosperity lies in increasing and diversifying its manufacturing industries as quickly as possible.

The text explains that agriculture and industry are not exclusive of each other.

The text explains that for instance, the agro-industries in India have given a major boost to agriculture by raising its productivity.

The text explains that they depend on the latter for raw materials and sell their products such as irrigation pumps, fertilisers, insecticides, pesticides, plastic and PVC pipes, machines and tools, etc. to the farmers.

The text explains that thus, development and competitiveness of On the occassion of Diwali, Harish went to a market with his parents.

The text explains that his mother purchased utensils, sugar, tea and diyas (earthen lamps).

The text explains that his father explained that shoes, clothes, sugar etc. are manufactured by machines in large industries, some utensils are manufactured in small industries, while items like diyas are made by individual artisans in household industry.

The text explains that industries may be classified as follows: On the basis of source of raw materials used: • Agro based: cotton, woollen, jute, silk textile, rubber and sugar, tea, coffee, edible oil. • Mineral based: iron and steel, cement, aluminium, machine tools, petrochemicals.

The text explains that according to their main role: • Basic or key industries are those which supply their products as raw materials to manufacture other goods e.g. iron and steel and copper smelting, aluminum smelting. • Consumer industries that produce goods for direct use by consumers – sugar, toothpaste, paper, sewing machines, fans etc.

The text explains that on the basis of ownership: • Public sector, owned and operated by government agencies – BHEL, SAIL etc. • Private sector industries owned and operated by individuals or a group of individuals –TISCO, Bajaj Auto Ltd., Dabur Industries. • Joint sector industries which are jointly run by the state and individuals or a group of individuals.

The text explains that they pool in the resources and share the profits or losses proportionately.

The text explains that (i) Oil (vi) Sewing Machines (ii) Knitting needles (vii) Shipbuilding (iii) Brassware (viii) Electric Bulbs (iv) Fuse wires (ix) Paint brushes (v) Watches (x) Automobiles Agro-based Industries Cotton, jute, silk, woollen textiles, sugar and edible oil, etc. industries are based on agricultural raw materials. manufacturing industry has not only assisted agriculturists in increasing their production but also made the production processes very efficient.

The text explains that 60 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II Textile Industry: The textile industry occupies unique position in the Indian economy, because it contributes significantly to industrial production, employment generation and foreign exchange earnings.

The text explains that 62 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II Mineral-based Industries Industries that use minerals and metals as raw materials are called mineral-based industries.

The text explains that factors responsible for their location in the Hugli basin are: proximity of the jute producing areas, inexpensive water transport, supported by a good network of railways, roadways and waterways to facilitate movement of raw material to the mills, abundant water for processing raw jute, cheap labour from West Bengal and adjoining states of Bihar, Odisha and Uttar Pradesh.

The text explains that 6.2 Processes of Manufacture of Steel Production and consumption of steel is often regarded as the index of a country’s development.

The text explains that bauxite, the raw material used in the smelters is a very bulky, dark reddish coloured rock.

The text explains that the flow chart given below shows the process of manufacturing aluminium.

The text explains that regular supply of electricity and an assured source of raw material at minimum cost are the two prime factors for location of the industry.

The text explains that basic chemicals undergo processing to further produce other chemicals that are used for industrial application, agriculture or directly for consumer markets.

The text explains that noise pollution not only results in irritation and anger, it can also cause hearing impairment, increased heart rate and blood pressure among other physiological effects.

The text explains that unwanted sound is an irritant and a source of stress.

The text explains that some suggestions are- (i) minimising use water for processing by reusing and recycling it in two or more successive stages (ii) harvesting of rainwater to meet water requirements (iii) treating hot water and effluents before releasing them in rivers and ponds.

The text explains that (b) Secondary treatment by biological process (c) Tertiary treatment by biological, chemical and physical processes.

The text explains that overdrawing of ground water reserves by industry where there is a threat to ground water resources also needs to be regulated legally.

The text explains that (c) Providing green belts for nurturing ecological balance and addressing the question of special purpose vehicles for afforestation.

The text explains that (d) Reducing environmental pollution through ash pond management, ash water recycling system and liquid waste management.

The text explains that it has ISO certification for EMS (Environment Management System) 14001.

The text explains that the corporation has a proactive approach for preserving the natural environment and resources like water, oil and gas and fuels in places where it is setting up power plants.

The text explains that write the answers of the following questions in 120 words.

The text explains that (ii) What are the other inputs in the process of manufacturing that involve transportation cost?

The text explains that textiles, sugar, vegetable oil and plantation industries deriving raw materials from agriculture are called… 2.

The text explains that this fibre is also known as the ‘Golden Fibre’.

The text explains that iron-ore, coking coal, and limestone are the chief raw materials of this industry.

The text explains that a public sector steel plant located in Chhattisgarh.

The text explains that 70 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II G G G P V A R A N A S I U O J I P G X K M Q W V K S U G A R C A N E E N O T T O N O Z V O P T R A U E L U B H I L A I U T K O C R A Q N T R L N E I R O N S T E E L S J E N A N O E P I T L R Y G A N U J D R A G D T A N T A R P O A P U E P Y A S N A E N J D I Y S K S M H V L I A J H S K G ACTIVITY Solve the puzzle by following your search horizontally and vertically to find the hidden answers.

The text explains that railway diesel engines are manufactured in Uttar Pradesh at this place.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II, Chapter 6: Manufacturing Industries

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
